caption: Fig. 1. (a) XRD patterns of the milled samples at various milling time; (b) Enlarged view in the range of 2θ (degree) = 49◦-54◦; (c) XRD pattern of the 35 h milled sample; (d) Rietveld refinement of the corresponding 35 h milled sample. This shows the alloying behavior of alloy as a function of milling time, and formation of the multi-phase structure (ferrite as a major phase) after 35 h of milling.

paper_title: Low-density $\mathrm { F e _ { 4 0 } M n _ { 1 9 } N i _ { 1 5 } A l _ { 1 5 } S i _ { 1 0 } C _ { 1 } }$ high entropy steel processed by mechanical alloying and spark plasma sintering: Phase evolution, microstructure and mechanical properties
citation_sentences: Phase evolution study of the milled samples are studied using XRD at various milling time, and it is shown in Fig. 1(a). The pre-mixed powder of alloying elements at 0 h reveals the diffraction peaks related to the

elements i.e., Fe (cI2, JCPDS# 00-006-0696), Mn (cI58, JCPDS# 00- 032-0637), Ni (cF4, JCPDS# 00-004-0850), Al (cF4, JCPDS# 00-004- 0787), Si (cF8, JCPDS# 00-005-0565) and C (hP4, JCPDS# 00-056- 0159). It is evident (from Fig. 1(a)) that the diffraction peak intensity of all the element decreases with the milling time. Low intensity peaks of all the elements have vanished after 5 h of milling. The intensity of Al diminishes after 15 h of milling indicating that Al has the highest alloying rate. After 20 h, intense peaks of Ni and Mn are not evident in the XRD pattern. The alloying of the elements with the milling time can be related to the diffusion of low melting point elements into the matrix of higher melting point elements, as reported previously [39]. The solid solution has been formed after 25 h of milling. The phases formed are BCC (a = 0.286 nm; JCPDS# 00-006-0696) and γ-brass type $( \mathbf { a } = 0 . 8 7 2$ nm; JCPDS# 01-078-2831) structure. The milling has been extended up to 35 ${ \bf h } ,$ for further nanostructuring of milled powder and formation of homogenous solid solution. There is a phase change i.e., B2-type ordered and coexisted with the other phases. Minor peak broadening and the decrease in the amount of silicon has occurred as milling extended to 35 h.

It is evident that the trace amount of Si remained even after 35 h of milling. The BCC as a major phase, γ-brass type and B2-type $( \mathsf { a } = 0 . 2 9 0$ nm, JCPDS# 01-083-3994) phases along with trace amount of Si were formed after 35 h of milling. In the 35 h milled powder, no signature of contamination was observed, as discerned through X-Ray energy dispersive spectroscopy. In our previous investigation on the Al matrix and quasicrystalline matrix composites milled for more than 50 h in WC vials and with WC balls, no signature of contamination was detected [40,41]. Fig. 1(b) illustrates the XRD pattern with the milling in the enlarged view of 2θ ranges from 49◦-54◦. For 35 h milled powder, XRD pattern is illustrated in Fig. 1(c) which shows the formation of multi-phase structure. Fig. 1(d) displays the Rietveld refinement plot of 35 h milled powder of the alloy. The standard peaks of the elements (Fe, Mn, Ni, Al and Si) and the XRD pattern of pre-milled powder in Supplementary Fig. S1 (a & b). Similarly, the simulated diffraction peaks of the three phases (BCC, B2-type and γ-brass type) along with the XRD pattern of 35 h milled powder is shown in Supplementary Fig. S2 (a & b). It is clearly evident from Fig. S1 many diffraction peaks of the alloying elements co-exist at the almost the same d-spacing. Similarly, simulated diffraction peaks for BCC, B2-type and γ-brass type (Fig. S2) discerns co-existence of diffractions peaks at almost same d-spacing.

The crystallite size and lattice strain of the milled samples were calculated using the Rietveld refinement method with the help of GSAS-II (Table 2). The crystallite size was found to vary as a function of milling (Table 2). The crystallite size was found to be tuned in the range of \~13–17 nm and \~10–13 nm for BCC and γ-brass type phase respectively. The crystallite size for Si decreased from 20 nm to 14 nm as milling extended from 25 h to 35 h. The lattice strain increased for all the phases as the milling time progressed. The increase in the lattice strain with milling is due to the atomic size difference between the individual elements, mechanical deformation in the powder and the increase in the fraction of grain boundary. Peak broadening and the decrease in diffraction peaks intensity with the milling are evident from Fig. 1(a). The reason is due to the lattice distortion leading to the induced lattice strain and extra grain refinement. The usual characteristics of the milling process and the combination of both the phenomenon contributes to the peak broadening. There is a slight change in the amount of phase fraction for the BCC and γ-brass type structure. The dislocation density shows the negligible change with the milling time and the values are displayed in Table 2.

caption: Fig. 2. (a–d) SEM micrographs showing morphology of powder samples milled at different milling time. Fig. 2(d) SEM micrograph showing 35 h milled powder particles used for the full area EDS analysis; (e) EDS spectrum of 35 h milled powder showing presence of different elements in the final composition; (f–i) Histograms showing the particle size distribution of milled samples at 10 h, 20 h, 30 h and 35 h, respectively; (j) SEM micrograph of the 35 h milled powder, marked for point EDS analysis and EDS mapping. The SEM micrograph illustrates the different contrasts i.e., light and dark grey corresponding to the BCC phase (rich in Fe) and γ-brass type structure (rich in Mn), respectively. The histogram shows the distribution of the powder particle with the milling time. The average powder particle decreases with the prolongs milling time.

paper_title: Low-density $\mathrm { F e _ { 4 0 } M n _ { 1 9 } N i _ { 1 5 } A l _ { 1 5 } S i _ { 1 0 } C _ { 1 } }$ high entropy steel processed by mechanical alloying and spark plasma sintering: Phase evolution, microstructure and mechanical properties
citation_sentences: Fig. 2(a–d) illustrates the size, shape and morphology of the milled powder at different milling time. Three different contrasts i.e., white, light and dark grey are clearly visible in the micrograph of 35 h milled sample. The size and shape of the powder particles are random and bulky at the early duration of the milling (as seen in Fig. 2(a)). The average particle sizes decreased drastically after 20 h of milling. The powder particles were becoming more homogenous as the milling increased. For 35 h milled samples, the mean particle size is 3–5 μm, and the size and shapes of the particles are different. The full area EDS analysis of the marked area (in Fig. 2(d)) is illustrated in Fig. 2(e), showing the peaks of all the elements in 35 h milled powder. Fig. 2(f–i) displays the histogram of particle size distribution at 10 h, 20 h, 30 h and 35 h, respectively. The particles size was measured using ImageJ software. Fig. 2(j) shows the microstructure of the powder pellet (35 h) and its corresponding mapping and the point EDS. Table 3 lists the elemental composition of 35 h milled powder from different areas i.e., full area, spot 1 and spot 2. The light grey contrasts are rich in Fe and it corresponds to BCC (ferritic phase) structure. The dark grey contrasts are rich in Mn and its stoichiometry are close to the γ-brass type structure. The chemical analysis also confirmed the formation of BCC (Fe-rich) and γ-brass type (Mn-rich) in 35-h milled powder.

caption: Fig. 3. (a) The TEM bright-field image of the 35 h milled sample; (b) its corresponding selected area diffraction pattern. Polycrystalline ring pattern corresponds to the BCC phase.

citation_sentences: The bright-field TEM image and its corresponding selected area diffraction pattern are shown in Fig. 3(a) and (b), respectively. The image shows that the nanocrystalline grains were formed after 35 h milled powder. The formation of ring pattern represents the polycrystalline nature of the alloy. The ring diffraction pattern correspond to the BCC crystal structure. The planes of (110), (200) and (211) of the BCC phase can be identified from the ring patterns.

paper_title: Low-density $\mathrm { F e _ { 4 0 } M n _ { 1 9 } N i _ { 1 5 } A l _ { 1 5 } S i _ { 1 0 } C _ { 1 } }$ high entropy steel processed by mechanical alloying and spark plasma sintering: Phase evolution, microstructure and mechanical properties
citation_sentences: The DTA curve of 35 h milled powder is displayed as Fig. 4, which reveals various exothermic events at 420 ◦C (693 K) and $5 2 0 ^ { \circ } \mathbf { C } ( 7 9 3 \mathbf { K } ) .$ . To understand the phase transformation of 35 h milled powder, the annealing was done at different temperatures of 400 ◦C, 500 ◦C, 600 ◦C and $8 0 0 ~ ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ followed by ex-situ XRD of annealed powders (Fig. 5 (a)). The enlarged view of the annealed sample in the 2θ range of 40◦- 46◦ is shown in Fig. 5(b). The deconvolution of phases around (110) peak of BCC phase for samples annealed $4 0 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } , 5 0 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } , 6 0 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } ,$ , and $8 0 0 ~ ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ are presented in Fig. 5(c), (d), (e), and $\left( \mathrm { f } \right) ,$ , respectively. The exothermic heating event observed at ${ \sim } 4 2 0 \ ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ may be corresponding to the ordering and/or texturing of γ-brass type phase $( \mathrm { F i g s . ~ } 4 ~ \& ~ 5 ( \mathrm { d } ) )$ . The heating event at ${ \sim } 5 2 0 ~ ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ corresponds to the formation of Fe5Si3-type phase (Figs. 4 & 5(e)). It is clear that 35 h milled powder is thermally stable up to $5 0 0 ~ ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } ,$ , and afterwards the diffraction peaks of the Fe5Si3 type intermetallic phase (hP16) is evolved at $6 0 0 ~ ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ and grows further up to $8 0 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } .$ At 800 ◦C, no phase change has occurred but the amount of the B2-type phase is increased. The phase fraction, crystallite size and lattice parameter of the phases evolved during annealing at different tempeatures were ascertained through rietveld refinement and are mentioned in Table 4.

caption: Fig. 5. (a) Ex-situ XRD diffraction pattern of the milled sample at R.T., 400 ◦C, 500 ◦C, 600 ◦C and 800 ◦C; (b) Enlarge view of diffraction peaks in 2θ range of 42◦- 46◦; (c–e) Deconvoluted peaks for the 400 ◦C, 500 ◦C and 600 ◦C, respectively; (f) Enlarged view of the 800 ◦C, ranging from 40◦- 46◦. The formation of Fe5Si3-type intermetallic phase at 600 ◦C is correlated with exothermic event at 520 ◦C (Fig. 4).

paper_title: Low-density $\mathrm { F e _ { 4 0 } M n _ { 1 9 } N i _ { 1 5 } A l _ { 1 5 } S i _ { 1 0 } C _ { 1 } }$ high entropy steel processed by mechanical alloying and spark plasma sintering: Phase evolution, microstructure and mechanical properties
citation_sentences: SPSed pellet of non-equiatomic HES, and the distribution of all the el ements are uniform and the chemically homogeneous. The BSE image and the EPMA elemental mapping of the SPSed sample reveals the multiphase nature of the non-equiatomic HES as shown in Fig. 8. The different contrast features are clearly visible in the BSE image i.e., white, light

Further, Fig. 8 represent the EPMA elemental maps of Fe, Mn, Ni, Al and Si. It can be discerned that the Fe, Mn and Ni was found to be almost uniformly distributed in the matrix owing to the exsistence of the major BCC phase. The high X-Ray intensity regions corresponding to the Al has slightly higher intensity of Fe and Ni, which may be attributed to the presence of B2-type phase. Similarly, the region enriched with Si shows the presence of Fe Si -type phase. It is very dificulty of distinguish the regions corresponding to the γ-brass type phases. This can be corroborated with the XRD results owing to the very less phase fraction of γ-brass type phases in SPSed samples. The EMPA elemental mapping helped in the understanding and presence of the multi-phase microstructure in these HES prepared by SPS (Fig. 8).

caption: Fig. 9. (a) Depth of penetration vs Indentation load (p-h plot); (b) Optical micrograph of the indent on SPSed HES sample; (c) Compressive engineering stress and strain curve; (d) Radar diagram showing comparision of different mechanical properties; (e and f) Comparition (microhardness vs density, and ultimate compressive stress vs density) of the present work with the conventional steel and other HEAs (details mentioned in supplementary Table S1). This illustrate the physical and mechanical properties of the SPSed pellet.

paper_title: Low-density $\mathrm { F e _ { 4 0 } M n _ { 1 9 } N i _ { 1 5 } A l _ { 1 5 } S i _ { 1 0 } C _ { 1 } }$ high entropy steel processed by mechanical alloying and spark plasma sintering: Phase evolution, microstructure and mechanical properties
citation_sentences: The load-displacement curve of the SPSed pellet of non-equiatomic high entropy steel is shown in Fig. 9(a). The nature of the p-h curve depicts the presence of limited plasticity in these sintered samples. The depth of indetation for the SPSed samples was found to be \~5.08 μm. Fig. 9(b) shows the optical image of indent on the surface of SPSed pellet. The optical micrograph shows no evidence of crack formation even at a load of 5000 mN. Fig. 9(c) display the compressive stress-strain plot of the SPSed sample. The evaluated values of the average microhardness and elastic modulus of the SPSed pellet are observed approximately $7 . 8 \pm 0 . 3$ GPa and $1 8 4 \pm 5 \mathrm { \ G P a } ,$ , respectively. Fig. 9(d) shows the radar diagram of the SPSed pellet revealing the physical and mechanical properties. The evaluated values of the average ultimate compressive stress and strain of the SPSed pellet are $2 0 4 6 \pm 1 6 0$ MPa and 19%, respectively. Fig. 9(e) and (f) show the comparative curve between microhardness and compressive strength vs theoretical density, respectively of the present alloy and the reported HEAs, conventional stainless steel, dual-phase steel and low-density steel.

A comprehensive comparison of the present alloy with the high entropy alloys is shown in Fig. 9(e and f). In addition, the comparison with the conventional stainless steel, dual phase steel and low-density steel is also shown. The ferritic dual phase steel has the microhardness in the range of 2.0–2.4 GPa [59]. The microhardness of the present alloy (7.8 GPa) is much higher than the dual-phase steel. The Fig. 9 (e and f) shows the comparison of different alloys with respect to theoretical density and microhardness with the reported HEAs [21,22,26,59,60–66], which are also mentioned in the Supplementary Table S1. The quinary low-density high entropy steel with the formation of BCC and B2 with silicide phase (intermetallic compound) showed the excellent mechanical properties i. $\mathrm { e } _ { \cdot , }$ compressive strength and microhardness as compared to the HEAs and conventionally steel. The present investigation envisaged to fabricate a low-density high entropy steel possessing good microstructural stability as well as excellent mechanical properties having synergy with ductility. In spite of having excellent microstructural stability and mechanical properties, these SPSed HES were found to have a limited ductility of \~19%. Further, efforts towards tailoring the composition for designing and developing a low-density HES having excellent room temperature and high temperature properties may be a way forward for its potential structural applications.
